prompts.url:
Seed indicator: type=url value=http://evil.com/x
This is a URL — derive the host (domain or IP) and investigate that as the
primary pivot, but keep the URL itself as a node in the graph.

STEP 1: add_node(url, <seed>, tags=["seed"])
STEP 2: Extract the host from the URL. If it is a domain, add_node(domain, <host>)
        and add_edge(url→domain, has_host). If it is an IP, add_node(ip, <host>)
        and add_edge(url→ip, has_host). Defuse the host before pivoting.
STEP 3: For the host, run the MANDATORY domain or IP workflow tools in full:
  - urlscan_search("page.url:http://evil.com/x") AND urlscan_search("domain:<host>")
  - urlhaus_host(<host>)
  - rdap_domain(<host>) / dns_resolve(<host>)   (or rdap_ip if host is an IP)
  - virustotal_domain(<host>) / virustotal_ip(<host>)
  - virustotal_communicating_files("domain"|"ip", <host>)
  - threatfox_search(http://evil.com/x)
  - otx_domain(<host>) / otx_ip(<host>)
STEP 4: Follow the similar-attack-pattern hunting steps (JARM, favicon,
        page.title, cert) on the host. Every finding becomes a node/edge.
STEP 5: Final report — use value="investigation_summary" and tie the URL
        seed to it with known_ioc.
prompts.ip:
Seed indicator: type=ip value=1.2.3.4
Investigate now. You MUST call ALL of these MANDATORY tools before writing the report:
1. rdap_ip(1.2.3.4)
2. virustotal_ip(1.2.3.4)  — extract JARM, cert SHA256/serial, issuer O=, malicious stats
3. shodan_host(1.2.3.4)  — extract JARM, open ports, banners, http_title
4. onyphe_ip(1.2.3.4)  — community-tier ok. Iterate the `digest` field:
   for each ip in digest.ips / jarm in digest.jarms / sub in digest.subdomains /
   feed in digest.threat_feeds → add_node + add_edge with source="onyphe".
5. urlscan_search("ip:1.2.3.4")
6. reverse_dns(1.2.3.4)
7. virustotal_resolutions_ip(1.2.3.4)
8. virustotal_communicating_files("ip", 1.2.3.4)
9. threatfox_search(1.2.3.4)
10. otx_ip(1.2.3.4)
BEST-EFFORT (call but skip cleanly if tier_restricted=true):
  - onyphe_threatlist(1.2.3.4)
  - onyphe_resolver_reverse(1.2.3.4)
JARM PIVOT (MANDATORY if a non-CDN JARM was extracted):
  - shodan_search("ssl.jarm:<jarm>")        (paid, may be tier_restricted)
  - onyphe_datascan("jarm:<jarm>")          (paid, may be tier_restricted)
  - urlscan_search("hash:<jarm>")           (FREE, ALWAYS call this)
  CLUSTER GRAPHING RULE: for EACH distinct IP in the union of shodan/onyphe/urlscan
  hits, add_node(ip, <ip>) + add_edge(seed→<ip>, same_jarm, source=<s|o|urlscan>).
  Graph the top 10 by ASN diversity. A prose summary without nodes is a graph failure.
CERT PIVOT (MANDATORY if virustotal_ip returned a cert serial or issuer.O):
  - crtsh_serial(<cert_serial>)  (FREE, always call). For each host in digest.hosts
    not already in graph: add_node(domain|ip, <h>) + add_edge(seed→<h>, same_cert,
    source="crtsh", evidence="crt.sh serial=<serial>").
  - If issuer.O is distinctive and not a CA (e.g. not DigiCert/LetsEncrypt/Sectigo/GoDaddy):
    crtsh_query("<issuer_O>", match="ILIKE")  → graph each new CN as above with same_cert.
FALLBACK: If virustotal_communicating_files returns empty data[] and threatfox/otx identify a specific malware family, call malwarebazaar_signature(<family>) and add each returned sample as a hash node with a communicates_with edge to the seed IP.
prompts.jarm:
Seed indicator: type=jarm value=aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa
This is a TLS JARM fingerprint. Follow the JARM workflow from the system prompt.
You MUST call ALL of these tools before writing the report:
1. add_node(jarm, aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa, tags=["seed"])
2. shodan_search("ssl.jarm:aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa")
3. urlscan_search("hash:aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa")
4. For top 3 diverse IPs (different ASN/org): defuse + rdap_ip + virustotal_ip + threatfox_search
5. threatfox_search(aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa)
Every host with the same JARM must be graphed (ip node + has_jarm edge).
If the cluster has >200 members, note 'common_jarm_likely_cdn' and keep 10 representatives.
Write the report last with value="investigation_summary".
prompts.asn:
Seed indicator: type=asn value=AS13335
This is an Autonomous System Number. Follow the ASN workflow from the system prompt.
You MUST call ALL of these tools before writing the report:
1. add_node(asn, AS13335, tags=["seed"])  (use the canonical AS<digits> form)
2. shodan_search("asn:AS13335 port:443")  — narrows to the web-facing slice
3. For top 5 most interesting IPs (unusual JARM / non-generic title / unusual ports):
   defuse + virustotal_ip + threatfox_search + otx_ip
4. rdap_ip on ONE representative IP from the ASN to capture netname/country/abuse_email
   MANDATORY: add_node(country, <ISO2>) + add_edge(asn→country, located_in)
5. threatfox_search("AS13335")
If multiple hosts inside the AS share the same JARM, graph the JARM node and link
every matching IP to it. Tag the asn 'abused_asn' when ≥2 hosts return detection hits.
Write the report last with value="investigation_summary".
prompts.executable_name:
Seed indicator: type=executable_name value=dropper.exe
This is JUST the filename of a malicious binary — the analyst does
NOT have the file itself and does NOT have its hash. Your job is to
find sample(s) ever reported under this filename and attribute the
family from there. There is no fingerprint to pivot on yet — the
filename is the only signal.

STEP 1: add_node(executable_name, dropper.exe, tags=["seed"], metadata={"extension": "<ext>"})
STEP 2: malwarebazaar_filename(dropper.exe)  — primary pivot. For EACH sample returned (up to the top 10, prioritising distinct
  sha256/signature/file_type triplets):
  - add_node(hash, <sha256_hash>, metadata={file_name, file_type, signature, first_seen}, source="malwarebazaar")
  - add_edge(<hash> → executable_name node, observed_as, source="malwarebazaar", evidence="reported with filename dropper.exe on MalwareBazaar")
  - If `signature` is set on the sample, that is the malware family
    — copy it onto the executable_name node as a tag (e.g.     'family:agenttesla', 'family:lummac2') and add an `attributed_to`
    relation in the report's metadata.
STEP 3: For the top 3 sample hashes (by recency / family diversity), run the full hash workflow:
  - virustotal_file(<h>)  — extract names[], meaningful_name, first_submission, family from popular_threat_classification
  - malwarebazaar_hash(<h>)  — yara/cape tags, C2 list
  - otx_file(<h>)  — pulse / actor attribution
  - threatfox_search(<h>)  — IOCs linked to that sample
  Graph every C2 / contacted_url / contacted_domain / contacted_ip the sample reveals (add_node + communicates_with edge).
STEP 4: threatfox_search(dropper.exe)  — sometimes ThreatFox
  entries reference filenames as IOCs (especially for droppers).
STEP 5: opencti_lookup_indicator(dropper.exe)  — community KG
  may have the filename indexed against an actor / campaign.
STEP 6: If no sample is found in MalwareBazaar AND threatfox finds
  no hit, the filename may be too generic ('update.exe',   'svchost.exe', 'taskmgr.exe') — note that explicitly in the
  report's metadata under `attribution_status: "filename_too_generic"` and STILL write a short investigation_summary.
STEP 7: Final report — value="investigation_summary", linking the
  executable_name node with known_ioc. The summary MUST state:
  - how many samples were found,
  - the dominant malware family (if any),
  - the most distinctive C2 / network IOC each family contacts,
  - whether the filename is generic / shared across families.
EXCEPTION: If malwarebazaar_filename returns zero samples and the
  filename matches a known legitimate binary (svchost, explorer,
  notepad, chrome, msedge…), tag the seed `generic_filename` and
  keep the report minimal — explain that the name alone is not a
  meaningful pivot.
prompts.email:
Seed indicator: type=email value=a@b.com
This is an email address — most often a malware/phishing registrant
contact, a C2 beacon target, an exfil drop, or a paste-site author.
Your job is to find every domain registered with this email, every
reputation signal, and any threat-intel mention.

STEP 1: add_node(email, a@b.com, tags=["seed"])
STEP 2: emailrep_check(a@b.com)  — reputation, disposable flag,
        spammer / malicious tags. Copy `details.profiles` onto the node
        metadata when present (linked social profiles).
STEP 3: whoxy_reverse(email="a@b.com")  — every domain ever
        registered with this email. For each returned domain (top 25
        prioritising recency / TLD diversity):
          - add_node(domain, <d>)
          - add_edge(email→domain, registered, source="whoxy")
        Tag the email `bulk_registrant` if ≥20 domains are returned.
STEP 4: pulsedive_indicator(a@b.com) — risk-scored corroboration.
STEP 5: opencti_lookup_indicator(a@b.com) — community KG hits.
STEP 6: threatfox_search(a@b.com) — listed as IOC?
STEP 7: For the TOP 3 domains discovered in STEP 3 (most recently
        registered or most-suspect TLD), run the full domain workflow
        (rdap, dns, VT, urlhaus, threatfox, otx) so the cluster has
        concrete infrastructure to pivot from.
STEP 8: Final report — value="investigation_summary". The summary
        MUST state: how many domains the email registered, whether the
        email is disposable / reputation-flagged, and the dominant
        malware family or campaign (if attributable).
prompts.wallet_address:
Seed indicator: type=wallet_address value=0xabc
This is a cryptocurrency wallet address — most often a ransomware
payment target, a scam-collection wallet, or a market vendor wallet.
We do not (yet) query block explorers — your job is to surface every
PUBLIC threat-intel mention of this address and graph the surrounding
infrastructure so analysts can attribute the campaign.

STEP 1: add_node(wallet_address, 0xabc, tags=["seed"], metadata={"chain": "<btc|eth|xmr|...>"})
        Set the chain in metadata based on the address format:
          - 0x + 40 hex   → ethereum (also BSC, Polygon — flag both)
          - bc1/tb1       → bitcoin (bech32)
          - 1 / 3 + b58   → bitcoin (legacy P2PKH / P2SH)
          - 4 / 8 + b58   → monero
STEP 2: threatfox_search(0xabc) — abuse.ch lists wallets in
        ransomware IOC bundles. Every matching threat_type / malware
        field becomes a tag on the wallet_address node.
STEP 3: pulsedive_indicator(0xabc) — risk + linked indicators.
STEP 4: opencti_lookup_indicator(0xabc) — community KG.
STEP 5: urlscan_search("0xabc") — sometimes the address shows
        up in scanned phishing page DOM (donate buttons, ransom notes).
        For each matching page graph it as a url node and tie back to
        the wallet with an embedded_in edge.
STEP 6: Final report — value="investigation_summary". The summary
        MUST state: the chain, whether the wallet has any direct
        threat-feed listing, and the campaign / malware family it is
        attributed to (if known). If NONE of the sources return a
        hit, set metadata.attribution_status="unattributed" and keep
        the report minimal. Without block-explorer access we cannot
        chain-trace — note that explicitly in `limitations`.
prompts.username:
Seed indicator: type=username value=h4ck3r
This is an actor handle / alias — could be a forum username, a
Telegram/X/GitHub handle, a malware-builder identifier, or a paste-
site author. Treat it as an opaque identifier and surface every
public mention in the threat-intel sources we have.

STEP 1: add_node(username, h4ck3r, tags=["seed"])
STEP 2: threatfox_search(h4ck3r) — sometimes lists known actor handles.
STEP 3: pulsedive_indicator(h4ck3r) — corroboration.
STEP 4: opencti_lookup_indicator(h4ck3r) — community KG.
STEP 5: urlscan_search("h4ck3r") — the handle may appear in
        page text on phishing kits or open-dir listings.
STEP 6: For every domain / IP / hash mentioned in returned records,
        graph it (add_node + uses_handle edge from the infrastructure
        back to the username node).
STEP 7: Final report — value="investigation_summary". The summary
        MUST state: which actor / campaign the handle attributes to
        (if any), how many concrete IOCs were tied to it, and what
        platforms / forums the handle has been observed on. If no
        public source mentions the handle, set
        metadata.attribution_status="no_public_record" and keep
        the report minimal — the handle alone is not actionable.
prompts.command_line:
Seed indicator: type=command_line value=powershell -enc Zm9v
This is a malicious command line / script / dropper snippet pasted by the analyst. The raw text is in the SOURCE REPORT block above — read it carefully BEFORE anything else.

STEP 1: add_node(command_line, powershell -enc Zm9v, tags=["seed"], metadata={"preview": "<first line>", "interpretation": "<one sentence: what does this command do>"})
STEP 2: Categorise the command. Pick one and add it as a tag:
  - powershell_dropper | bash_dropper | living_off_the_land | lolbins | base64_loader | hta_dropper | mshta_dropper | certutil_download | bitsadmin | curl_pipe_bash | iex_download | obfuscated_script
STEP 3: Identify EVERY embedded indicator and graph it as its own node:
  - URLs (curl/wget/Invoke-WebRequest/DownloadString targets) → add_node(url, <url>), add_edge(command_line→url, embedded_in_command)
  - IPs / domains → add_node + same edge
  - Hashes → add_node(hash, <h>) + same edge
  - Base64 blobs that decode to URLs/IPs → decode mentally, add the
    decoded indicator as a node + edge with evidence="decoded from
    base64 within command line".
  - LOLBin names (rundll32, mshta, regsvr32, certutil, bitsadmin,
    msbuild, installutil, …) → tag the command_line node with the
    lolbin name; no separate node needed.
STEP 4: For each embedded URL / domain / IP, run its standard
  workflow (urlscan_search + urlhaus_host + virustotal_* + threatfox_search).
STEP 5: If a binary hash is referenced or downloaded, run
  virustotal_file(<h>) + malwarebazaar_hash(<h>) + otx_file(<h>) to
  identify the family.
STEP 6: Final report — value="investigation_summary", linking the
  command_line node with known_ioc. The summary MUST describe what
  the command does AND which family / actor the embedded infrastructure
  belongs to (if attributable).
prompts.domain:
Seed indicator: type=domain value=evil.com
Investigate now. MANDATORY tools (must all run before the report):
1. rdap_domain/dns_resolve(evil.com)
2. crtsh_subdomains(evil.com)
3. virustotal_domain(evil.com)  — extract JARM, last_analysis_stats, categories
4. virustotal_resolutions_domain(evil.com)  — historical IPs
5. virustotal_communicating_files("domain", evil.com)
6. onyphe_domain(evil.com)  — community-tier ok. Iterate digest:
   for each ip in digest.ips / jarm in digest.jarms / sub in digest.subdomains /
   feed in digest.threat_feeds → add_node + add_edge with source="onyphe".
7. threatfox_search(evil.com)
8. otx_domain(evil.com)
BEST-EFFORT (call but skip cleanly if tier_restricted=true):
  - onyphe_ctl(evil.com)  — CT log SANs (each new → add_node(domain)+same_cert edge)
  - onyphe_resolver_forward(evil.com)  — alt-pDNS
JARM / FAVICON pivots (if extracted and not a CDN value):
  - shodan_search("ssl.jarm:<jarm>") and onyphe_datascan("jarm:<jarm>")
  - shodan_search("http.favicon.hash:<hash>") and onyphe_datascan("favicon:<hash>")
  Graph every cluster IP with a same_jarm/same_favicon edge. If BOTH sources return
  tier_restricted=true, note it in pivot_suggestions and keep going.
EXCEPTION: If step 1 shows the domain is clearly parked (parking NS + broker registrant), skip steps 2-8 and write a minimal report.
FALLBACK: If communicating_files returns empty data[] and OTX/threatfox identifies a malware family, call malwarebazaar_signature(<family>) to find known samples and add them as hash nodes.
